current_fact.fact_text: On March 20, 1616, Sir Walter Raleigh walked out of the Tower of London after 13 years as a prisoner. Once a celebrated explorer and court favorite, he had spent over a decade in stone walls contemplating lost voyages and half‑imagined continents. His release felt like an old map being opened again, even though the world he returned to had changed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

current_fact.match_reason: Raleigh’s release after long confinement echoes the episode’s theme of emerging from constriction to immerse again in the wider world—trading stone walls for open seas and deep experience.

queue.fact_text: In 1933, Heinrich Himmler ordered the creation of Dachau, one of the first Nazi concentration camps, near Munich. Initially presented as a place for ‘political prisoners,’ it became a brutal prototype for an entire system of terror, forced labor, and mass murder. Dachau’s gates marked the point where cruelty was industrialized and bureaucracy learned to manage horror.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: Dachau is a stark reminder of mechanized inhumanity; the dark, reflective atmospheres of classic trip‑hop in this episode offer a contemplative, somber soundscape that suits remembering heavy history without glamorizing it.

queue.fact_text: On March 20, 1848, amid the turbulent German revolutions of 1848–49, King Ludwig I of Bavaria abdicated his throne. Pressure from liberal uprisings, scandal over his private life, and demands for a constitution pushed a monarch to step aside in favor of his son. It was a rare moment when street protests and new ideas about citizenship shook a royal crown loose.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: A king forced to abdicate during revolutionary fervor and women seizing space in rock both challenge old hierarchies—one in politics, the other in culture—echoing the spirit of upheaval and rebalancing power.

queue.fact_text: On March 20, 2015, Kurdish YPG fighters and allied Free Syrian Army units broke the months‑long Siege of Kobanê in northern Syria. The small city had become a global symbol of resistance against ISIS, with its defenders fighting street by street against overwhelming odds. When the siege finally lifted, it marked not just a military turning point but a powerful story of local communities resisting annihilation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: The defense of Kobanê and Tuareg desert blues both arise from peoples in harsh landscapes whose music and struggle are intertwined with resistance, exile, and survival on the margins.

queue.fact_text: On March 20, 2014, four gunmen linked to the Taliban slipped into Kabul’s luxury Serena Hotel and opened fire, killing at least nine people, including children and foreign nationals. The attack transformed a supposed safe haven in the Afghan capital into yet another frontline, revealing how fragile the boundary was between war zones and daily life. Even spaces of celebration and diplomacy were suddenly marked by bullet holes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: The Serena Hotel attack shatters the illusion of safe travel and neutral hotels; this travel‑themed episode, with its reflective tone, invites listeners to think more deeply about movement, safety, and the privileges and risks of crossing borders.

queue.fact_text: On March 20, 2000, Jamil Abdullah Al‑Amin—once H. Rap Brown, a fiery Black Panther leader of the 1960s—was captured after the killing of sheriff’s deputy Ricky Kinchen and the wounding of Deputy Aldranon English in Atlanta. His arrest closed a long arc from civil‑rights street battles to decades of relative quiet as a Muslim community leader. The story remains a knot of activism, violence, state power, and contested narratives of justice.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: H. Rap Brown/Jamil Al‑Amin’s life moves through radical labels, reinvention, and heavy consequences; this episode’s focus on identity, labels, and self‑definition through conscious rap resonates with that complex, boxed‑in history.

queue.fact_text: On March 20, 1990, Imelda Marcos—the famously shoe‑collecting widow of former Philippine president Ferdinand Marcos—went on trial in New York for bribery, embezzlement, and racketeering. The woman who once waltzed through palaces and shopping sprees now faced decades of possible prison time and the scrutiny of detailed bank records. Her case turned opulence itself into a kind of evidence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: Imelda Marcos’s polished public image obscured deep corruption, much like Auto‑Tune can mask imperfections; the episode’s examination of what is hidden, corrected, or exaggerated parallels the spectacle and artifice of her lifestyle and trial.

queue.fact_text: On the night of March 20, 1861, a powerful earthquake struck Mendoza, Argentina, virtually destroying the city. Buildings of adobe and unreinforced brick crumbled, and fires swept through the ruins, killing thousands. In the aftermath, Mendoza was rebuilt with wider streets and new building codes, turning disaster into a harsh lesson in urban resilience.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: The Mendoza quake forced a city to rethink foundations and structures; this training‑and‑discipline‑oriented episode parallels how repeated blows, movement, and rebuilding—whether in a body or a city—create new strength after shock.

queue.fact_text: On March 20, 2010, Eyjafjallajökull’s eruption began under a glacier—ice on top, magma below—creating a rare blend of fire and frozen water. Steam explosions blasted ash high into the atmosphere, turning Europe’s busy skies into a quiet, airplane‑free void. It was a reminder that under many peaceful landscapes lurk forces just waiting to remix earth, air, and time.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A sudden volcanic eruption is nature’s own ‘electric shock’ to the atmosphere; this high‑energy, guitar‑driven episode matches the raw, explosive power of magma meeting sky.

queue.fact_text: On March 20, 2015, as the Sun dimmed in eclipse and the Moon swung close in supermoon phase, the Earth also reached equinox—day and night nearly equal worldwide. For a brief moment, skywatchers could sense multiple cosmic cycles intersecting: lunar orbit, Earth’s tilt, and our path around the Sun. It turned a simple date on a calendar into a multi‑layered celestial alignment.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: This rare triple sky event invites stillness and sky‑gazing; the ‘Slow Planet’ episode offers a calm, downtempo soundtrack perfectly suited to watching shadows move and senses recalibrate to cosmic time.

queue.fact_text: On March 20, 2016 (near this date’s orbit), many northern cultures mark the spring equinox with festivals that celebrate balance—light and dark held in temporary truce. Ancient monuments from Stonehenge to Mayan pyramids were designed to catch this moment with aligning shadows. It’s a yearly reminder that planetary mechanics are quietly choreographing our seasons and rituals.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

queue.match_reason: The equinox tips us toward longer days and the promise of summer; this sun‑drenched, jazz‑infused episode leans into that seasonal shift and the feeling of endless light.

queue.fact_text: On March 20, 2019 and every year around this date, countless musicians, dancers, and listeners unknowingly honor the American Federation of Musicians and other unions that once fought to protect performers—sometimes by banning recordings and broadcasts outright. The 1940s recording bans, including those lifted by 1948’s first TV orchestral broadcasts, forced labels to value players’ labor and led to new payment models. Every spin, stream, and sample today quietly carries the echo of those battles over who gets paid when music travels.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: AFM’s fight over how musicians are heard and paid sits behind every modern broadcast; Sedna FM’s own first ‘life soundtrack’ episode is a love letter to recorded music itself, made possible by those earlier struggles.

published.fact_text: In 1916, Albert Einstein quietly handed in a paper that rewrote the universe: “The Foundation of the General Theory of Relativity.” In it, gravity stopped being an invisible force and became the curvature of spacetime itself, meaning planets and light rays follow the bends and folds of the cosmos. The idea was so radical that it took years of astronomical tests to prove that starlight really does curve around the Sun.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: Both the fact and the episode reframe ‘empty space’ as something dynamic and creative—relativity literally curves spacetime, while Space Shift invites listeners to see emptiness as room to create.

published.fact_text: On March 20, 2015, three celestial events stacked on the same day: a solar eclipse, the March equinox, and a supermoon. For a few eerie minutes, parts of the Earth saw the Sun blotted out while the Moon was unusually close and large, exactly as day and night poised in perfect balance. It was a kind of cosmic choreography you could mark on a calendar but still feel as pure magic.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: The rare triple alignment in the sky pairs with a ‘cosmic’ dance-floor journey; both evoke celestial timing, cycles, and a sense of space-age wonder.

published.fact_text: In 1964, Europe decided to pool its curiosity about the cosmos and founded the European Space Research Organisation (ESRO), the ancestor of today’s European Space Agency. Instead of competing, nations shared rockets, satellites, and data, slowly building a common ladder into orbit. Many of Europe’s first scientific satellites—and the habit of exploring space together—began with this quiet agreement.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: ESRO represents an international voyage into space, and this episode is literally framed as a collective journey to a distant planet—both are about shared exploration beyond Earth.

published.fact_text: In 1948, after a long Musicians Union ban, American television finally broadcast live classical music, conducted by Eugene Ormandy and Arturo Toscanini. For many people, it was the first time orchestral sound leapt straight from a living room screen instead of a concert hall. That night quietly turned TV from a novelty into a gateway for high art.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: The first televised classical performances and this episode’s neo-classical, cinematic mood both capture how orchestral-style music can travel into new spaces—whether through early television or underwater daydreams in Arctic seas.

published.fact_text: On March 20, 1923, a private club in Chicago quietly staged Pablo Picasso’s first U.S. exhibition, modestly titled “Original Drawings by Pablo Picasso.” In a country still skeptical of cubist faces and fractured guitars, the Arts Club of Chicago became an unlikely portal for modern art. That show helped plant the seeds for America’s later fascination with European avant‑garde painting.

episode.title: Sedna FM #03 - Beautiful as You Feel
episode.description: An episode about self-acceptance and inner beauty. Music that reminds us we are beautiful as we feel, embracing our authentic selves. Warm, uplifting sounds that celebrate individuality.
songs: The The - this is the day
DCTL - Deep Tannin f(eat Rafael Leafar)

published.match_reason: Picasso’s daring, fragmented portraits challenged traditional beauty, just as this episode celebrates inner beauty and individuality beyond conventional forms.

published.fact_text: In 1903, the first auctions of sheep‑farming land in southern Patagonia began reshaping one of the wildest corners of South America. Vast estancias were carved out of windswept steppe, bringing fortunes to some and displacement to Indigenous communities and older settlers. The quiet bleating of sheep masked a dramatic transformation in land, power, and ecology at the tip of the world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #29 - Berber France Meets Arabic Italy
episode.description: Yasmin takes us back to her university years, where she met Abdel - an Algerian-French friend with a big heart and endless optimism. Their friendship grew around music, identity, and playful arguments about whether a song was originally Italian or French. Music that exists in both versions, alongside stories of finding a home, boxing training, and car rides filled with music.

published.match_reason: The Patagonian land auctions are about migration, identity, and who gets to call a landscape home; this episode centers on cross‑border identities and belonging, echoing those quiet questions of place and ownership.

published.fact_text: On March 20, 2010, Iceland’s Eyjafjallajökull volcano began an eruption that would spew ash into the sky for months, grounding thousands of flights across Europe. Airspace became an invisible no‑go zone, while stranded travelers improvised epic overland journeys. A single glacier‑topped volcano briefly reminded a whole continent that nature still writes the rules of the timetable.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A disruptive volcanic cloud and weather‑soaked memories in the episode both show how sky and climate can upend plans, yet the episode’s hopeful tone mirrors the resilience people found while grounded by ash.

published.fact_text: In 1987, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved AZT, the first drug shown to slow HIV, in a record 20 months. At a time when AIDS felt like an unstoppable sentence, this antiretroviral offered a fragile but real foothold in the fight. It wasn’t a cure, but it proved that science—and relentless activism—could change the trajectory of a pandemic.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: AZT’s approval came after intense research, trial, error, and activism; the episode’s focus on persistence and the slow, effortful process of learning mirrors the scientific struggle behind that breakthrough.

published.fact_text: On March 20, 1815, Napoleon Bonaparte marched back into Paris after escaping exile on the island of Elba, greeted by cheering crowds and rapidly swelling ranks. In a matter of days, a deposed emperor rebuilt an army of hundreds of thousands, setting the stage for his dramatic final act known as the “Hundred Days.” Europe collectively held its breath as history tried to rewind itself.

episode.title: Morning Drops #30 - Star Treatment
episode.description: A celebration of change and transformation. Gaia traces the evolution of Arctic Monkeys - from raw energy to cinematic sound - reflecting on how artists, and all of us, grow and change through time. A journey through courage, curiosity, and the beauty of slowing down.
songs: Arctic Monkeys - There’d Better Be a Mirrorball

published.match_reason: Napoleon’s sudden return is a drastic ‘second act’ for a public figure; this episode meditates on transformation, shifting personas, and how narratives change over time, echoing that intense reinvention.

published.fact_text: In 1942, having been ordered to leave the Philippines, U.S. General Douglas MacArthur arrived in Terowie, South Australia, and made a promise that would echo through the rest of the war: “I came out of Bataan and I shall return.” The line was broadcast worldwide, turning a retreat into a vow and a rallying cry for eventual liberation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: MacArthur’s defiant pledge to return and Rage Against the Machine’s politically charged anthems both turn words into weapons of resistance and morale.

published.fact_text: On March 20, 1922, the USS Langley was commissioned as the first aircraft carrier of the United States Navy. A converted coal ship with a flat wooden deck, it looked experimental and almost improvised, yet it quietly invented the idea of a floating airbase. Nearly every modern carrier—and many geopolitical strategies—trace back to that odd, pioneering vessel.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: The Langley was literally a new kind of ship changing life at sea; this boat‑based episode, steeped in ocean travel and floating communities, mirrors that sense of life lived on the water and in motion.

published.fact_text: In 1921, residents of Upper Silesia went to the polls in a plebiscite mandated by the Treaty of Versailles to decide whether their industrial homeland would belong to Germany or Poland. Families, languages, and loyalties were often mixed within the same street, turning a simple ‘yes or no’ vote into a deeply personal choice. The outcome shaped borders—but also left scars of identities split by lines on a map.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

published.match_reason: A border‑defining vote in Upper Silesia and a wandering journey through modern Poland both explore how geography, identity, and everyday life cross political lines.

published.fact_text: On March 20, 1616, Sir Walter Raleigh walked out of the Tower of London after 13 years as a prisoner. Once a celebrated explorer and court favorite, he had spent over a decade in stone walls contemplating lost voyages and half‑imagined continents. His release felt like an old map being opened again, even though the world he returned to had changed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: Raleigh’s release after long confinement echoes the episode’s theme of emerging from constriction to immerse again in the wider world—trading stone walls for open seas and deep experience.
